author: @totherarf
text:
There are problems with the design of cyclone vacs like you are using ...... primarily small storage volume and awkward emptying!

You can get round this easily by adding a Dust Collector to the hose. There are many designs of them but one of the simpler ones is to bolt a Dust Deputy type cyclone (available from about £9 upwards) to a circular plate that fits onto a bucket. The dust and chips (your vac will not do chips well at all) will collect in the bucket and, when full you simply unclip the plate and take the bucket for emptying ... no dust ... no mess .... and it would make a good video!

If you do not like the idea of paying for a Dust Devil (and who does?) you could make a Thane Baffle easily in your shop!

Eather would make a good video ..... and expand the use of recycled to include bagged vacs as they rarely need emptying! 

If you do try a Thane Baffle and you make the outer shell from perspex you get the added value of watching the waste spiraling into the collector .... it is almost worth making the mess just to watch the magic of physics in action ;0)

author: @BoB4jjjjs
text: We had to sand down wood without making much of a mess, so we used a vacuum cleaner like you did, except, it was not clean enough, so we got a Tee Piece, put it o the cleaner (we had to adapt the pipe size and I can't remember how we did it),  We put one end into the sander, one end into the vacuum cleaner and made a pipe up to go to the rear, we then fitted the small fanned out tool that came with the vacuum cleaner so it was close to the sanding belt (we used quite a few fittings to get it round, later we got a plastic pipe heat bent) and got it as close without obstructing the sander.  The belt sander throws out most at the rear due to the rotation, so most of the dust gets picked up.  We did have one of the industrial vacuum cleaners with normal and high suction.  It worked very well.  It was in a hospital and although staff came in to clean every night we got no complaints about dust.  It was close to a sensitive area and they had to keep it running.  The part we used at the rear of the cleaner was just about the same size as the sander, so it could not have been better.

author: @scantrain5007
text:
Dear Luke,
the best of this is you used a cyclon vacuum cleaner, where the dust is not ending in a paper bag which gets dispose in the bin. Don't know if you do gardening ... but if you mix the wood dust with soil (earth) you save a bit dropping garbage and help plants growing in the garden. 
Just try it - and see (in a few weeks) what's happend!

replies.author: @barbufodor1186
replies.text: I would first make sure  the wood was not treated.

replies.author: @stephenowens5375
replies.text: I've used an old Kirby in the past, it was excellent. And yes wood dust is excellent in the garden but be careful on a veg patch, many woods especially from pallets contain serious toxins 😉. Great comment all the same.

replies.author: @stevetobias4890
replies.text: You can use a bucket that has a pipe on a sharp angle on the top side then a hose from the lid to the tool. The suction creates a cyclonic action and gravity allows most of the dust to fall to the bottom of the bucket instead of into the vacuum cleaner bag / collector.

replies.author: @garethb5729
replies.text:
I never put wood in my soil. Too much carbon. 
It works like a nutrient sponge. 
Also the promotion of fungus growth isn't helpful.

replies.author: @MyNameIsLukey
replies.text: That's a really cool idea, I don't do really any gardening but that is something I'm interested in, learning to grow my own crops. If I do something like that in the futre I will definitely use this tip. Thanks mate! :)
